name: Temptation Bundling
tagline: Pair something you should do with something you want to do.

origin.source: Milkman, Minson & Volpp (2014). Holding the Hunger Games hostage at the gym. Management Science

situations: Bingeing a guilty pleasure you'd rather limit
Struggling to stick with a habit
Wanting to make a chore tolerable
summary: Temptation bundling links an activity you should do but avoid with one you love but indulge in too much — you only allow the want while doing the should. Milkman's gym study let people listen to addictive audiobooks only while exercising, and attendance jumped. It solves two problems at once.
howItWorks: We over-discount delayed rewards (the health benefit of exercise) and over-weight immediate ones (a gripping audiobook). Bundling brings an immediate, desirable reward into the moment of the difficult behavior, so the should now carries instant payoff. It also adds a layer of self-control by restricting the temptation to only happen alongside the beneficial activity.

steps.instruction: Identify a 'should' you keep avoiding (exercise, chores, admin).
Identify a 'want' you enjoy, maybe a little too much (a show, podcast, treat).
Make a rule: the want is only allowed during the should.
steps.note: e.g. 'I only watch my favorite series while on the treadmill.'

steps.instruction: Protect the rule — the bundle only works if the want stays exclusive to it.

whenToUse: When a beneficial habit feels too boring to sustain
When a pleasure has become a time-sink
Making chores or admin more bearable
Building consistency where willpower keeps failing
whenNotToUse: When the 'should' needs full attention and the 'want' distracts dangerously (e.g. driving)
If the temptation is genuinely harmful and should be reduced, not scheduled
When bundling makes you resent both activities
tips: Keep the want exclusive to the bundle or the effect fades
Pick a want compatible with the should (audio for movement)
Re-bundle if it stops feeling rewarding
Combine with an implementation intention for the cue

evidence.summary: A randomized field experiment found temptation bundling increased gym attendance; subsequent studies support its effect on adherence to beneficial behaviors.
citations: Milkman, K.L., Minson, J.A. & Volpp, K.G. (2014). Holding the Hunger Games hostage at the gym. Management Science.
